title: St. Fabian of Rome, St. Demetrius, St. Porphyrius of Gaza & St. Julian of Emesa
primary_saint: St. Fabian Pope of Rome, St. Demetrius the Great Martyr, St. Porphyrius of Gaza, and St. Julian of Emesa
narrative:
On the eleventh day of Yekatit, the holy Church gathers four witnesses who illuminate different aspects of the Church's passage through violence and power: the bishop who was chosen by a dove and died by a sword, the soldier-martyr who became a patron of cities, the bishop who faced down a pagan mob, and the martyr of a provincial city whose story the world has nearly forgotten. Saint Fabian, chosen as Bishop of Rome by the miraculous descent of a dove upon his head while he stood as an obscure layman in the assembly, governed the Roman church for fourteen years of peaceful consolidation before the Decian persecution swept him to martyrdom. His election is preserved in the Ethiopian Synaxarium as a teaching about divine initiative in the Church's life: that the Spirit's choice does not follow human expectation, that the lowly are raised and the expected are passed over, and that the sign that authenticates episcopal authority is not lineage or learning but the manifest grace of God.

Saint Demetrius of Thessalonica is the great soldier-martyr of the Greek world, the patron of that city and of a military tradition of Christian piety that spans the whole Byzantine and post-Byzantine era. Like the two Theodores commemorated earlier in this month, Demetrius was a Roman officer who refused to persecute Christians, was imprisoned, and was martyred while in his prison cell by soldiers who thrust their spears through the bars. The tradition elaborates his story with accounts of miraculous intervention — of his appearance in battle to defend his city against barbarian attack, of the oil that flows from his relics — but the heart of his witness is the simple refusal of the powerful man to use his power against the innocent, and the willingness to die for that refusal.

Saint Porphyrius of Gaza is a figure of extraordinary pastoral courage: appointed bishop of the overwhelmingly pagan city of Gaza at the end of the fourth century, he found himself responsible for a tiny Christian community surrounded by an antagonistic majority whose great temple of Marneion dominated the city's religious and civic life. Porphyrius travelled to Constantinople, obtained an imperial rescript ordering the temple's destruction, and returned to Gaza to face the riots that followed, protecting his flock through years of violence and slowly, painfully, building a Christian community in one of the most resistant cities of the East. His biography, written by his deacon Mark, is one of the most vivid documents of the confrontation between Christianity and classical paganism, and the Ethiopian Church receives him as a model of episcopal persistence in the face of seemingly impossible odds.

Saint Julian of Emesa is this day's figure of discovery — a martyr of the Syrian city of Emesa (modern Homs) whose witness was characterised by a quality the tradition calls holy simplicity: he was a man of no great education or social standing, who confessed Christ before the magistrate with a directness and serenity that confounded the court's attempts to entrap him in legal technicalities. Asked why he was not afraid of death, Julian replied that he had been dying to himself for many years and had nothing left to lose. He was executed, and his relics were carried to the Church of Emesa where they became a source of healing. The tradition's preservation of Julian alongside Fabian, Demetrius, and Porphyrius is a reminder that the great episcopal administrators and the celebrated soldier-patrons are finally united with the ordinary believer who dies simply, without ceremony, asking nothing but to see the face of God. Amen.
